SACRAMENTO
1020 N STREET, SUITE 107
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-4154

SAN FRANCISCO
50 FREMONT STREET, SUITE 1403
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94105
(415) 396-9880

BRAD SHERMAN
901 WILSHIRE BLVD., SUITE 210
SANTA MONICA, CA 90401
(310) 451-5777
(818) 360-3186
FACSIMILE (310) 458-9088

CHAIRMAN,
STATE BOARD OF EQUALIZATION

MEMBER,
FRANCHISE TAX BOARD

December 21, 1992

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: Request for Advice --
Mass Mailing Provisions of Political Reform Act

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

Pursuant to 2 CCR section 18329, subdivision (b), the undersigned, with authorization from and on behalf of Brad Sherman, Member, State Board of Equalization, hereby requests formal written advice regarding the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Does the attached sample meet the requirements of the "constituent meeting notice" exemption to the mass mailing ban, such that, under the circumstances described herein, more than 200 of them may be mailed in a calendar month to the constituents of Mr. Sherman, Member, Board of Equalization, Second District?

1. Does the attached sample meet the requirements of FPPC regulation 18901, subdivision (b)(9)?

Mr. Sherman intends to hold several constituent meetings during 1993 for Board of Equalization permit holders, and tax accountants², each of whom would be mailed a notice identical (except for the precise date, time and location of the meeting) to Exhibit A attached hereto. The meetings will be public ones at which Mr. Sherman will attend and discuss various tax administration issues facing the Board of Equalization. We respectfully submit that such meetings fall squarely within the ambit of Regulation 18901, subdivision (b)(9)(A)(1), which reads as follows:

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

² The term "tax accountant" includes tax return preparers licensed by the California Department of Consumer Affairs, public accountants licensed by the California State Board of Accountancy, and Enrolled Agents licensed or enrolled by the Internal Revenue Service.

An announcement sent to an elected officer's constituents concerning a
public meeting which is directly related to the elected officer's incumbent
governmental duties, which is to be held by the elected officer, and which
the elected officer intends to attend.

And, we respectfully submit that Exhibit A fulfills the requirements of Regulation
18901, subdivision (b)(9)(B), which provides as follows:

Any announcement provided for in this subdivision (b)(9) shall not include
the elected officer's photograph or signature and may include only a single
mention of the elected officer's name except as permitted elsewhere in this
regulation.

Exhibit A does not include Mr. Sherman's photograph or signature, and mentions the
name of "Brad Sherman" only once.

2. May more than 200 of Exhibit A be mailed to individuals residing in or
doing business in the existing Equalization Second District to which he was elected in
November 1990?

In its February 4, 1992 Milman Advice Letter (No. I-91-57), the Commission
concluded that "the persons residing or doing business in the elected officer's existing
district continue to be the officer's constituents for purposes of the exception in
Regulation 18901(b)(9)(A)(1) until the next election." Id., page 5.

In the November 1990 general election, Mr. Sherman was elected to represent the
Second Equalization District, as such district is defined in Elections Code section 30042.
Mr. Sherman will represent the district as defined in Elections Code section 30042 until
January 1995.

We respectfully submit that the Commission's Milman advice letter authorizes the
mailing of more than 200 of Exhibit A in a calendar month, as long as such mailing is
limited to individuals or businesses with addresses in the 16 full counties and/or Los
Angeles County census tracts listed in Elections Code section 30042.

3. May more than 200 of Exhibit A be mailed to individuals residing in or
doing business in any of the three newly reapportioned Equalization Districts in which
Mr. Sherman may run as an incumbent in the 1994 election?

In Wilson v. Eu, (1992) 1 Cal. 4th 707, the California Supreme Court
reapportioned the four Board of Equalization districts, all of which will elect a Member
in the 1994 election, such that the existing Second Equalization District to which Mr.
Sherman was elected in 1990 overlaps three of the four new Equalization Districts drawn
by the Supreme Court.³

The new First Equalization District includes the following counties from the
existing Second Equalization District: San Francisco, San Mateo, Monterey, San Benito,
and Santa Cruz.

³ A copy of the statewide map, and county and Los Angeles County census tract list from the Special
Master's Report, is attached hereto as Exhibit B.

This "overlap area" between the new First Equalization District and the existing Second Equalization District contains over 1.5 million residents.

The new Second Equalization District includes the following counties from the existing Second Equalization District: Fresno, Kings, Madera, Mariposa, Merced, Santa Barbara, San Joaquin, San Luis Obispo, Stanislaus, Tulare and Ventura. In addition, the new Second Equalization District also includes the following Los Angeles County census tracts from the existing Second Equalization District: 9010.02, 9008.02, 9008.01, 9104.00, 9007.02, 9002.00, 9005.00, 9006.03, 9006.02, 9003.00, 9007.01, 9101.00, 9105.00, 9107.02, 9006.01, 9106.00, 9107.01, 9109.00, 9110.00, 9100.00, 9001.00, 9103.00, 9011.00, 9010.01, 9012.02, 9012.01, 9102.00, 9108.02, and 9009.00. This "overlap area" between the new Second Equalization District and the existing Second Equalization District contains over 1.5 million residents.

The new Fourth Equalization District includes the following Los Angeles County census tracts from the existing Second Equalization District: 1011 through 1436.02, inclusive; 1438.01 through 1439.02, inclusive; 2611.01 through 2674.02, inclusive; 2679; 2714; 2731 through 2739, inclusive; 3201 through 3203, inclusive; 7006 through 7023, inclusive; and 8001.00 through 8005.02, inclusive. This "overlap area" between the new Fourth Equalization District and the existing Second Equalization District contains over 1.5 million residents.

In the Milman advice letter, the Commission concluded: "...during the interim period after reapportionment and before the November elections, any residents in geographic territory which has been shifted to the district for which the assembly member qualifies as an incumbent under Elections Code section 10212 are also the assembly member's constituents."
(Emphasis added)

As indicated above, Board of Equalization members will not be elected from the new Equalization Districts until 1994.

a. Mr. Sherman may qualify as the "incumbent" in the new Second Equalization District.

Elections Code section 10212 provides that, "[a]t the first elections for ... Members of the Board of Equalization in each ... Board of Equalization district following reapportionment ...", the following analysis shall be used to determine which candidate "shall be deemed the incumbent in a given district for purposes of the election".

Subdivisions (a) and (b) of Elections Code section 10211 require that the candidate be running for reelection to the office which he then holds in a district with identical boundaries as the district from which he was last elected. Obviously, no current Member of the Board of Equalization satisfies this requirement.

Subdivision (c) provides that: "In the event there is no candidate to whom the provisions of subdivision (a) or (b) apply, the incumbent shall be that candidate who is running for the same office he then holds and who is running for reelection in a district which has the identical number as the district from which he was last elected..."

Thus, Mr. Sherman is the only Board of Equalization Member who may qualify as
an "incumbent" in the new Second Equalization District."

We respectfully submit that under the analysis of the Milman advice letter, more
than 200 of Exhibit A may be sent in the same calendar month to individuals residing in
or doing business in the new Second Equalization District.

b. The Milman advice letter also permits Exhibit A to be mailed to individuals
residing in or doing business in the new First and Fourth Equalization Districts.

As noted above, the conclusion of the Milman advice letter is the "...during the
interim period after reapportionment and before the November elections, any residents
in geographic territory which has been shifted to the district for which the assembly
member qualifies as an incumbent under Elections Code section 10212 are also the
assembly member's constituents."
(Emphasis added)

Mr. Sherman clearly qualifies as "an incumbent" under Elections Code section
10212, subdivision (d). That subdivision provides that:

In the event there is no candidate to whom the provisions of subdivision
(a), (b), or (c) apply, the incumbent shall be that candidate who is running
for the same office which he then holds and who is running for reelection
in a district which contains some portion of the territory previously
contained within the district from which he was last elected;...

As indicated above, both the new First Equalization District and the new Fourth
Equalization District contain "some portion of the territory previously contained within
the district" from which Mr. Sherman was elected in 1990, the existing Second
Equalization District defined in Elections Code section 30042. In fact, in each case the
"overlap portion" between either the new First or new Fourth districts and the current
Second Equalization District contains over 1.5 million residents.

Please do not hesitate to contact me at (310) 451-5777 if you have any further
questions.

Very truly yours,

John Thiella
Chief of Staff

Encl. -- 2

CHAIRMAN OF THE STATE BOARD OF EQUALIZATION
(California's Elected Tax Commission)

will speak on

California Sales and Income Tax Law --
Building a Partnership With Business

And Will Meet With You and Other
Business People and Tax Professionals

Representatives of the IRS, the California Franchise Tax Board, and the Tax Branch of the California
Employment Development Department will also attend and answer your questions.

This is your opportunity to express your concerns and recommendations
regarding state taxation and to get expert answers to your tax questions.

[Date]

[Time]

[Location]

[Parking information]

For more information, call ____________ at (310) 451-5777.

— FREE —

California Board of Equalization Districts

Board of Equalization

District One consists of Del Norte, Siskiyou, Modoc, Humboldt,
Trinity, Shasta, Lassen, Tehama, Mendocino, Glenn, Lake, Colusa,
Sutter, Sonoma, Napa, Yolo, Solano, Marin, Contra Costa, San
Francisco, Alameda, San Mateo, Santa Clara, Santa Cruz, San Benito
and Monterey counties.

District Four consists of all of Los Angeles County not included in
either District Two or District Three.


District Two consists of Plumas, Butte, Yuba, Sierra, Nevada,
Placer, El Dorado, Sacramento, Amador, Alpine, San Joaquin,
Calaveras, Tuolumne, Stanislaus, Mariposa, Mono, Merced, Madera,
Fresno, Inyo, Kings, Tulare, San Luis Obispo, Kern, San Bernardino,

We respectfully submit that such meetings fall squarely within the ambit of Regulation 18901, subdivision (b)(9)(A)(1), which reads as follows: --- ¹ Government Code sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. ² The term "tax accountant" includes tax return preparers licensed by the California Department of Consumer Affairs, public accountants licensed by the California State Board of Accountancy, and Enrolled Agents licensed or enrolled by the Internal Revenue Service.
